jsonLdWeb.name: Wood Fence Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate posts, rails, pickets, concrete, and hardware for wood fence projects.

mainEntity.name: How many fence pickets do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Divide the total fence length in inches by the picket width plus gap. For 100 ft of privacy fence with 3.5" pickets and no gap: 1,200 inches ÷ 3.5" = 343 pickets. With 1/4" gap: 1,200 ÷ 3.75 = 320 pickets. Add 10% for waste.

mainEntity.name: How far apart should fence posts be?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard spacing is 6 or 8 feet on center. 6-foot spacing is more common and provides better structural support, especially for tall fences (6 ft+) or in high-wind areas. 8-foot spacing works for shorter fences in calm areas. Gate posts should be no more than 4 feet apart for a single gate.

mainEntity.name: How many rails per fence section?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use 2 rails for fences 4 feet and under, and 3 rails for fences 5 feet and taller. The top rail sits 6-8 inches from the top, the bottom rail 6-8 inches from the bottom, and the middle rail is centered between them.

mainEntity.name: How much concrete per fence post?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a 4×4 post in a 10" hole at 24" deep, plan 1-2 bags of 80 lb concrete per post. Most standard fence projects use about 1.5 bags per post. End posts and gate posts should use slightly more.

Board-on-board (also called shadowbox) uses overlapping pickets on alternating sides of the rails. It provides privacy from direct angles while allowing airflow. It uses about 50% more pickets than a standard privacy fence because boards overlap by about half their width.

mainEntity.name: How many nails or screws per picket?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use 2 fasteners per picket per rail. For a 6-foot fence with 3 rails, that's 6 nails or screws per picket. Use hot-dipped galvanized or stainless steel fasteners to prevent rust stains. Ring-shank nails hold better than smooth nails.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Wood Fence Materials

The calculator divides fence length by post spacing for posts, multiplies sections by rails per section, and divides total length by picket width + gap for pickets.

Pickets = Fence Length (in) ÷ (Picket Width + Gap)
  • Posts: fence length ÷ post spacing + 1
  • Rails per section: 2 for fences ≤ 4 ft, 3 for fences ≥ 5 ft
  • Pickets: total fence length (inches) ÷ (picket width + gap)
  • Board-on-board: multiply pickets by ~1.5 for overlap
  • Concrete: ~1.5 bags of 80 lb per post
  • Fasteners: 2 per picket per rail (6 per picket for 3-rail fence)
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many pickets for my fence?

Divide total fence length (in inches) by picket width plus gap. For 100 ft with 3.5" pickets and no gap: 1,200 ÷ 3.5 = 343 pickets. Add 10% for waste.

How far apart should posts be?

6 ft is standard and best for tall or wind-exposed fences. 8 ft works for short fences in calm areas. Gate posts should be no more than 4 ft apart.

How many rails per section?

2 rails for 4 ft and under, 3 rails for 5 ft and taller. Top and bottom rails sit 6-8" from fence edges; middle rail is centered.

How much concrete per fence post?

About 1.5 bags of 80 lb concrete per post for a 4×4 post in a 10" hole at 24" deep. End and gate posts may need 2 bags.

What is board-on-board?

Overlapping pickets on alternating sides of rails for privacy with airflow. Uses about 50% more pickets than standard privacy fence.

How many nails per picket?

2 fasteners per picket per rail. For a 3-rail fence: 6 per picket. Use galvanized or stainless steel to prevent rust.
